query: where do hematopoietic stem cells originate in humans

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Hematopoiesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hematopoiesis is the process of creating a wide variety of blood and bone marrow cells, namely erythrocytes, platelets, granulocytes, lymphocytes, and monocytes. This process begins with multipotent hematopoietic stem cells (HSC) which have the capability of dividing into either a multipotent progenitor cell or to self-renew. Progenitor cells are then able to divide into increasing specialized cells, a process which repeats and eventually leads to mature white blood cells, red blood cells, or platelets.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Hematopoiesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Multi-potent progenitors and any cells the progenitors create lack the capacity to self-renew and must instead always divide into a further specialized cell. Which type of cell the HSCs and progenitor cells divide into is largely decided by the specific signaling factors such as erythropoietin, Interleukin (IL) -2, IL-3, IL-6, IL-7, a variety of colony stimulating factors, and the, niche that the HSC develops in. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Hematopoiesis -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Novel cell lineage tracking technologies have led to recent revisions in the model of hematopoiesis. Classically, the model is as described above: HSCs divide into multipotent progenitor cells that can further divide into common myeloid progenitors or common lymphoid progenitors. Revised models demonstrate fewer branch points and instead show a steady differentiation of heterogenous HSC to the final cell lines. This leads to a to a more varied pool of HSC that are partially differentiated as opposed to groups of identical precursors. Traditionally these HSC are differentiated by cell surface markers which vary between HSC, progenitor cells, and mature cells. Cell surface markers between these partially differentiated HSC do not necessarily change despite a change in gene signaling, which gave rise to the classical branching model. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation -- Introduction -- Engraftment
retrieved_docs.content: The process by which infused transplanted hematopoietic stem cells produce mature progeny in the peripheral circulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation -- Introduction -- History and Evolution
retrieved_docs.content: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HPSCT) was first explored for use in humans in the 1950s. It was based on observational studies in mice models, which showed that infusion of healthy bone marrow components into a myelosuppressed bone marrow could induce recovery of its function in the recipient. [1] These animal-based studies soon found their clinical application in humans when the first successful bone marrow transplant was performed between monozygotic twins in New York in 1957 to treat acute leukemia. [2] The performing physician, E. Donnell Thomas, continued his research on the development of bone marrow transplantation and later received the Nobel Prize for Physiology and Medicine for his work. The first successful allogeneic bone marrow transplant was reported in Minnesota in 1968 for a pediatric patient with severe combined immunodeficiency syndrome. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Hematopoiesis -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: In postnatal life, definitive erythropoiesis occurs exclusively in the bone marrow under normal physiologic conditions. In infants, RBC production takes place throughout the spongy and trabecular bone. In adults, however, erythropoiesis becomes restricted to the axial skeleton—specifically the vertebrae, sternum, ribs, and proximal ends of long bones. Within the bone marrow, HSCs generate all mature hematopoietic lineages through a hierarchy of intermediate progenitors, which will be discussed in the following section. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Hematopoiesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: This series of divisions in hematopoiesis creates a chart with several branch points beginning with the multipotent progenitor cells dividing into either a common myeloid progenitor or a common lymphoid progenitor. Common myeloid progenitors eventually go on to create megakaryocytes, erythrocytes, basophils, neutrophils, eosinophils, and monocytes. Common lymphoid progenitors will produce Natural Killer cells as well as B and T lymphocytes. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Hematopoiesis -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Soon after EryP development, highly proliferative, MPPs known as high proliferative potential colony-forming cells (HPP-CFC) emerge in the yolk sac. These cells initiate the 1st wave of definitive hematopoiesis and give rise to EryD. Sometimes referred to as erythroid/myeloid progenitors, these cells serve as a transitional population between primitive erythropoiesis and hematopoiesis derived from hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs). These progenitors migrate to and colonize the fetal liver, the next definitive hematopoietic site during gestation.

retrieved_docs.title: Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation -- Introduction -- History and Evolution
retrieved_docs.content: Since then, allogeneic and autologous stem cell transplants have increased in the United States (US) and worldwide. The Center for International Blood and Marrow Transplant Research (CIBMTR) reported over 8000 allogeneic transplants performed in the US in 2016, with an even greater number of autologous transplants; autologous transplants have steadily outpaced allogeneic transplants over time. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Pure Red Cell Aplasia -- Introduction -- The Hematopoietic Process and Erythrocyte Formation
retrieved_docs.content: All cells in the peripheral blood and some solid tissues, eg, osteoclasts, arise from hematopoietic stem cells. Hundreds of billions of blood cells can form daily from a stem cell pool of only 100,000. Hematopoietic stem cells can undergo self-renewal and differentiation, allowing continuous blood cell regeneration and maintenance.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Hematopoiesis -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: By the 7th week of gestation, HSCs migrate to and colonize the fetal liver, where they proliferate and begin to differentiate. At this stage, the liver becomes the dominant site of hematopoiesis. HSCs also transiently colonize the spleen around the 20th week, contributing to erythropoiesis for a brief period. Simultaneously, HSCs begin to seed the bone marrow—a critical transition, as the bone marrow eventually becomes the primary site of erythropoiesis as gestation progresses.

retrieved_docs.title: Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Special equipment exists for collecting, preserving, and administering stem cell products.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Hematopoiesis -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The fetal liver provides a supportive microenvironment that promotes HSC expansion and differentiation through a hierarchical progenitor system. EryD cells arise from these progenitors. EryD cells rapidly surpass EryP in circulation and become the predominant erythroid population.

retrieved_docs.title: Pure Red Cell Aplasia -- Introduction -- The Hematopoietic Process and Erythrocyte Formation
retrieved_docs.content: Blood cells appear in the 3rd week of embryonic development in the yolk sac. By the 3rd month, hematopoietic stem cells translocate from the yolk sac to the liver, the main site of blood cell production until the antenatal period. In the 4th month, hematopoietic stem cells start to populate various bone marrow sites. At birth, all skeletal marrow sites are hematopoietically active, replacing the liver and becoming the only blood cell source. The marrow remains red and hematopoietically active until puberty. By age 18, about half of the marrow spaces become fatty and inactive, and the only active sites remaining are found in the skull, vertebrae, sternum, ribs, pelvis, and proximal humeral and femoral epiphyses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1739
retrieved_docs.content: Bone marrow transplant is the term that classifies the Arabic phrase زراعة نخاع العظم, indicating that both expressions refer to the same medical procedure of transplanting hematopoietic stem cells from donor bone marrow, thereby linking the English and Arabic terminology for this treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2697
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “cancer; other and unspecified primary” includes malignant blood tumors (الأورام الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) as a member of this group, indicating that these hematologic malignancies are categorized under cancers of other or unspecified primary origin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation of فقر دم فانكوني (Fanconi anemia), indicating that the clinical and laboratory findings associated with this genetic disorder are considered abnormal. Fanconi anemia is a hereditary condition characterized by bone‑marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and a heightened risk of malignancies, and the descriptor “Abnormal” reflects the pathological nature of its presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 55
retrieved_docs.content: support to immune‑modulating virotherapy—used for the treatment of hematologic malignancies such as leukemia and other cancers, while also identifying specific contraindications and adjunctive uses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cells that define the disease known as سرطانة العظم (bone cancer).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1761
retrieved_docs.content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is classified as a type of تسريب, which itself is a kind of نقل الدم (blood transfusion) and belongs to the broader category of الإجراءات العلاجية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر (therapeutic procedures not classified elsewhere). In addition to its relationship with تسريب, INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is also linked to فصادة الكريات البيض (white‑blood‑cell apheresis) and to نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), indicating that it shares characteristics with both apheresis procedures and self‑donated blood transfusion. نقل الدم encompasses نقل الدم الذاتي as a specific child category, and the term blood transfusion is used to classify نقل الدم. Blood transfusion is associated with complications of surgical procedures or medical care, highlighting that transfusion practices can be a cause of such complications. Together, these relationships illustrate how various procedural categories—INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS, تسريب, فصادة الكريات البيض, and نقل الدم الذاتي—are interconnected within the overarching framework of blood‑related therapeutic interventions and their potential impact on patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 239

Collectively, these relationships delineate a hierarchical taxonomy in which lymph‑node neoplasms, their classification under Hodgkin’s disease and unspecified primary cancers, their anatomical context within the hematopoietic‑lymphatic system, and their patterns of spread to lymph nodes are integrated with broader categories of thoracic and blood‑derived malignancies, providing a comprehensive framework for clinical understanding and patient‑care decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: central nodes that connect specific lymph‑node cancers (سرطان العقد اللمفاوية), broader unspecified primary cancers, metastatic pathways to lymph nodes and bone, and related thoracic and hematologic malignancies, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding the taxonomy and clinical implications of lymph‑node and related neoplastic diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2820
retrieved_docs.content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS are classified as subprocedures within the broader categories of فصادة الكريات البيض (white‑blood‑cell apheresis) and نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion). Both parent procedures involve the manipulation, collection, or reinfusion of blood components, and the inclusion of INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS under these headings underscores their role in delivering therapeutic agents or devices directly into the patient’s circulatory system or tissues as part of blood‑related therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 873
retrieved_docs.content: Neoplasm of lymph node encompasses the specific entity ورم الغدد الليمفاوية, which represents a lymph‑node tumor. This tumor is anatomically linked to the Hematopoietic and Lymphatic System, reflecting the system’s role as the primary site associated with the development of such lymph‑node neoplasms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2698
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical classification of neoplasms of the hematopoietic and immune systems encompasses Hodgkin’s disease, indicating that Hodgkin’s disease is categorized as a specific type of hematopoietic and immune system neoplasm within this classification framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES is classified under the broader category إجراء تدخلي, indicating that it is an interventional procedure. Within this group, the specific procedure صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية is considered a child or sub‑procedure, representing a spinal cord marrow imaging technique that falls under the miscellaneous diagnostic and therapeutic procedures umbrella.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ category of immune disorders/autoimmune comprises a diverse group of hematologic and rheumatologic conditions. Within this category, aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is recognized as an autoimmune-mediated failure of bone‑marrow production. Non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) is also classified as an autoimmune disorder in which the marrow does not adequately replace red blood cells. Acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) and its cold‑agglutinin subtype (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد) are both autoimmune hemolytic processes in which antibodies target red blood cells, leading to their premature destruction. In addition, inflammatory arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) is included as an autoimmune joint disease characterized by chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Together, these entities illustrate how autoimmune mechanisms can affect both blood formation and joint health, underscoring the importance of recognizing their shared immunologic basis for accurate diagnosis and coordinated patient management.
